coredata.dc:title: Development of carbon composite coatings for fire retardancy and electromagnetic interference shielding
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, Pradeep Kumar
Tasi, Tsung-Pin
Wu, Min-Wei
Dash, Pranjyan
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Yang, Po-Chih
Chang, Jeng-Kuei
coredata.dc:description: This study has effectively showcased the feasibility of employing a carbon composite coatings, comprised of various carbon nanomaterials including carbon nanodots, carbon nanotubes, and graphene nanosheets integrated within a matrix of sodium metasilicate + gypsum, for applications in both flame retardancy and electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding. The resulting carbon composite coatings, when applied to wooden substrates, demonstrate remarkable fireproof performance, as validated through fire injections at temperatures as high as 1050 °C. This underscores the pivotal role plays by the design of carbon composites in suppressing flame propagation and charring, leading to reduced carbonization areas. Thermogravimetric analysis further reveals that the carbon composite coating generates substantial char residue at 800 °C, signifying enhanced thermal insulating properties derived from the robust composite carbon architecture. Furthermore, the carbon composite coatings show a significant decrease in electromagnetic fields (electric field around 50 % and magnetic field around 44 %), indicating their efficient EMI shielding properties. This improved EMI shielding performance may be due to the deliberate insertion of CNs into the composite materials, which fine-tunes resistance and dielectric loss while allowing effective electromagnetic absorption by the carbon composites. The breakthroughs made in flame retardancy and EMI shielding highlight carbon composites as superior strengthens to flame-retardant and EMI-absorbing building coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Fire retardancy
Carbon nanocomposites
Electromagnetic interference shielding
Wooden substrate
Construction coating

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fireproof performance of carbon composite coatings
EMI shielding performance of carbon composite coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs of (a) CNs, (b) CNTs, (c) CNT + GN, and (d) CN + CNT + GN composites.
Typical XRD patterns of different coatings.
Top-view FE-SEM images of (a) blank and (b) C-012 sample and its corresponding elemental mapping.
Photographs of different carbon coatings on wooden substrates after the fire injection test.
TGA curves and their derivatives weight as a function of temperature: (a) C-01, (b) C-02, (c) C-012, and (d) C-12 coating.
The carbonized area as a function of fire injection time.
(a) Magnetic, and (b) electric field shielded by different carbon coatings.
(a) The shielding efficiency of magnetic and electric field by different carbon coatings, and (b) magnetic and electric field shielded by C-012 coating with different thicknesses.

head.ce:title: Development of carbon composite coatings for fire retardancy and electromagnetic interference shielding

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 30010, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study has effectively showcased the feasibility of employing a carbon composite coatings, comprised of various carbon nanomaterials including carbon nanodots, carbon nanotubes, and graphene nanosheets integrated within a matrix of sodium metasilicate + gypsum, for applications in both flame retardancy and electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding. The resulting carbon composite coatings, when applied to wooden substrates, demonstrate remarkable fireproof performance, as validated through fire injections at temperatures as high as 1050 °C. This underscores the pivotal role plays by the design of carbon composites in suppressing flame propagation and charring, leading to reduced carbonization areas. Thermogravimetric analysis further reveals that the carbon composite coating generates substantial char residue at 800 °C, signifying enhanced thermal insulating properties derived from the robust composite carbon architecture. Furthermore, the carbon composite coatings show a significant decrease in electromagnetic fields (electric field around 50 % and magnetic field around 44 %), indicating their efficient EMI shielding properties. This improved EMI shielding performance may be due to the deliberate insertion of CNs into the composite materials, which fine-tunes resistance and dielectric loss while allowing effective electromagnetic absorption by the carbon composites. The breakthroughs made in flame retardancy and EMI shielding highlight carbon composites as superior strengthens to flame-retardant and EMI-absorbing building coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood has evolved as a popular material in recent years, with substantial applications, notably in the building industry. The increasing inclination towards wood can be justified by its exceptional mechanical and structural characteristics, most notably its extraordinary strength-to-weight ratio [1,2]. Wood is an ideal material for a vast array of construction applications, including innovative architectural designs, commercial complexes, and residential housing. This is due to its adaptability and inherent strength. Nevertheless, wood is not without its inherent dangers, most notably the potential for fire damage, which could significantly undermine its structural integrity and overall functionality. Ensuring the protection of wood from the detrimental effects of fire becomes an extremely important priority, motivated by factors including the preservation of lives, ecological health, and structural integrity. The substances intended to protect wood from the destructive effects of fire are frequently referred to as fireproofing compounds. These compounds are crucial in enhancing the fire resistance of wood. They accomplish this through a complex mechanism that entails reducing the amount of heat released during the early stages of combustion, preventing the release of smoke and volatile substances, and impeding the spread of flames across the material's surface [3]. By optimizing this multifunctional approach, the objective is to increase the fire resistance of wood, thereby safeguarding its functionality and safety profile across a range of applications.

Generally, flame retardants are used as an additive in polymers, fibers, and coating materials to increase their thermal stability and resistance towards the fire [4,5]. Flame retardants are thus activated by ignition source and prevent the growth of fire by the elimination of any one of these key components [6]. Graphene nanosheets (GNs) and graphene are two-dimensional (2D) nanocrystals with single or several atomic layers consisting of sp2-bonded carbon structure [7–9]. Prior applications of graphene materials have been successfully demonstrated for the transparent conducting films [10], catalyst supports [11–13], flow batteries [14], lithium-ion batteries [15], and electrochemical capacitors [16]. Our previous study has pointed out that functionalized GN-based coating imparts a satisfactory thermal resistance to improve the fire retardancy of wooden substrates [17]. Differential scanning calorimetry revealed that the exothermic peak contains temperature delay in the presence of GNs, primarily due to the formation of a thermal barrier. Nonetheless, carbon allotropes beyond the conventional forms, including zero- (0D) and one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures like carbon nanodots (CNs) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have found limited application within the realm of anti-flammability coatings, as evident from the existing literature. Our groundbreaking investigations have showcased the efficacy of catalytic chemical vapor deposition [18,19], and microwave-assisted pyrolysis methods [20–22] in the synthesis of CNTs and CNs, respectively. Herein the as-prepared CNTs and CNs could serve as 0D and 1D fillers, respectively, which are expected to facilitate the fire retardancy. Due to their high thermal stability (i.e., CNs, CNTs, and GNs), large specific surface adsorption capability and strong barrier effect, the unique design of hierarchical structured composites are demonstrated to effectively alleviate heat and mass transfer against thermal damage [23–27]. The carbon composite coatings are expected to thus generate a barrier layer against oxygen [28], reducing oxygen access as well as heat transfer rate within the interfaces [29–32].

Besides anti-flammability, carbonaceous materials (e.g., CNTs and carbon fibers) also display great excitement because of their potential applications in the fields of electrical and thermal conductive composites, hydrogen storage, energy conversion systems, integrated circuits, microwave-absorption, and electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding [33–40]. If possible, light weight and broadband absorption is the significant consideration in designing EMI shielding and microwave-absorption materials [41,42]. Thus, carbonaceous composites can be considered as not only fire-retardant fillers but also EMI absorbers through the unique and robust design of carbon composite coating. However, the literature of carbon hetero-structures rarely reports their EMI shielding and microwave-absorption properties, which have been a great concern in society for the protection of the environment and human race from radiation emitting by cell phones and other telecommunication equipment [43–46].

In line with this, the present study employs an advanced homogenization technique to introduce an innovative category of environmentally benign, halogen-free, and minimally toxic carbon composite-infused flame retardant and EMI shielding coating onto wooden substrates. With the aim of mitigating fire hazards in construction structures, heterogeneous carbon composite-filled coatings, encompassing combinations like sodium metasilicate and gypsum, were applied onto the wooden substrates. A pivotal determinant for enhancing the fire-resistant attributes of these construction coatings on wood was identified to be the specific amalgamation of carbon structures, encompassing 0D and 1D configurations, 2D structures, or their hybrid combinations. The significant improvement in fire resistance of the treated wooden substrates is confirmed by validation via direct heating experiments. It was noted that the wooden substrate underwent dehydration as a result of the combustion process, which discharged CO2 and H2O and culminated in the formation of a substance resembling char. Additionally, a thorough investigation was conducted to determine the effect of carbon structure on the efficacy of EMI shielding. The knowledge acquired from this study illuminates the potential of carbon composites as beneficial candidates for multifunctional construction coatings; they possess remarkable properties including fire resistance and EMI shielding capabilities.

The 0D (CNs), 1D (CNTs), and 2D (GNs) carbon nanomaterials used in this investigation and synthesized in the manner described in the preceding section. First, the CN products were developed by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and urea (99 %, SHOWA, Japan) at 250 °C in a microwave. The weight ratio of citric acid/urea was fixed at 2:1, and the microwave power was 720 W for 10 min. The microwave-assisted synthesis method was similar to the solid-phase microwave-assisted method in our previous study [21,22]. The CNT products were grown by catalytic chemical vapor deposition at 900 °C, where NiO (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and acetylene (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) (Ar:H2:C2H2 = 94:1:5 in v/v/v) served as catalyst and carbon precursor, respectively [19]. The GN samples were exfoliated from natural graphite powders (Sigma-Aldrich, Japan) by the modified Hummers' method, as previously described [47].

These carbon nanomaterials were homogeneously dispersed in ethanol solution through an ultrasonic bathing technique. Herein, four combinations of carbon composite materials including 0D + 1D, 0D + 2D, 0D + 1D + 2D, and 1D + 2D, were prepared, designated to C-01, C-02, C-012, and C-12, respectively. The as-prepared carbon composites were then mixed via the equal weight ratio, and the liquid mixture (solvent: ethylene glycol) was placed into a high-performance homogenizer (IKA, Model T25, Germany). The homogenizing dispersion process was carried out at ambient temperature for 0.5 h, ensuring the formation of homogeneous hybrids. After that, the carbon composites were separated using filtering apparatus, and then dried at 105 °C in a vacuum oven for overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fireproof performance of carbon composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the fire-retardant performance, the fireproof coating (including sodium metasilicate + gypsum) mixed with four types of carbon composites (i.e., C-01, C-02, C-012, and C-12 samples) were prepared to coat over wooden plate. Herein, each fireproof coating consisted of 20 mL of sodium metasilicate, 0.408 g of gypsum, and 1.8 g of carbon composite. The wooden plates (i.e., three plywood) were carefully cut into a rectangle shape (ca. 3 × 4 cm2) with the average thickness of ~0.8 cm. The fireproof slurries were then well dispersed and then stirred by a magnetic bar at 160 rpm overnight. The as-prepared slurries were coated on the wooden substrates with a doctor blade and then dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. The thickness of each carbon composite coating was well controlled within 0.3 mm. To facilitate a comparative analysis, we also prepared the base fireproof coating, consisting of sodium metasilicate and gypsum, in order to assess its fire-retardant properties.

The flame retardancy of the construction coatings on wooden plates was evaluated by using a high-performance flamethrower. The distance between the coating and the top of flame was set at 2 cm, where the surface temperature on the wooden plates was kept at 1050 °C. In the fireproof test system, three thermocouples (K type) were adopted to measure the surface temperatures of the plates. The burnt area ratio was calculated by dividing its plank area (i.e., 12 cm2). The procedure was identical to the “BS 476: Part 7” standard for examining for anti-flammability of CaCO3 plates [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EMI shielding performance of carbon composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effectiveness of EMI shielding, uniform layers of the carbon composite were applied evenly onto wallpaper substrates. The thickness of each carbon composite coating was meticulously controlled using doctor blade method to be within a range of 0.12 mm. These carbon-coated papers were then meticulously cut and fashioned into rectangular boxes for the EMI shielding measurements. In order to establish a baseline, an electrical appliance (utility frequency: 60 Hz) emitting an electric field of 350 V/m and a magnetic field of 43 × 10−6 T was activated, serving as a reference background. The methodology employed for measuring anti-electromagnetic wave performance involved the use of a specific electrical appliance, which was entirely enveloped by the carbon-coated wallpaper. Throughout the measurements, the diminished electric and electromagnetic fields were recorded at regular intervals of 5 s, and this process was repeated for a total of 10 runs. Subsequently, both sets of values were collected and averaged to derive the final measurement results.

The morphology and structure of carbon composites were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-5600) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). Thermo gravimetric analyzer (TGA, Perkin Elmer TA7) technique was adopted to inspect the thermal stability of carbon coatings. The TGA analysis was conducted under the air atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, ramping from 50 °C to 800 °C. The crystalline structural analysis of carbon composites was performed through X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brucker D2 diffractometer equipped with Cu target).

TEM micrographs of the as-prepared CNs, CNTs, CNT/GN composite, and CN/CNT/GN composite are thoughtfully presented in Fig. 1 . Upon close examination, it is evident that the CN sample assumes a spherical morphology with an average diameter measuring 3.5 nm, showcasing a uniform dispersion throughout. In contrast, the CNT sample exhibits a tubular structure characterized by a remarkably high aspect ratio exceeding 100. These structural attributes designate CNs as 0D nanomaterials and CNTs as 1D nanomaterials. The CNT/GN composite reveals an intricate configuration where coiled CNTs are interwoven within the 2D graphene sheets, collectively forming a carbon composite network. On the other hand, the CN/CNT/GN hybrid is notably distinguished by the abundant presence of CNs and CNTs seamlessly integrated within the interleaved graphene sheets, resulting in the creation of a complex carbon composite framework. These observations illuminate the diverse structural characteristics of these nanomaterials and their combinations, setting the stage for a comprehensive understanding of their applications and properties.

Fig. 2 illustrates the characteristic XRD patterns of various fireproof coatings, including the blank (sodium metasilicate + gypsum) and those containing carbon samples (sodium metasilicate + gypsum + carbon composite). In the blank sample, no prominent peaks are discernible. Nevertheless, a faint, low-intensity, broad peak is evident in the range of approximately 20–23°, indicating the presence of a non-crystalline silicon dioxide phase, primarily arising from the incorporation of sodium metasilicate (i.e., polymeric metasilicate anions [–SiO2−₃]ₙ). These observations align with prior research findings [48]. Additionally, distinct peaks at around 10 and 20° can be attributed to the formation of amorphous calcium sulfate (i.e., CaSO4·2H2O), stemming from the inclusion of gypsum [49]. In contrast, the carbon-inclusive samples exhibit a discernible peak at roughly 26°, which corresponds to the graphitic structure of carbon composites [50], especially when highly crystalline CNTs are employed. Conversely, the other two carbon additives, CNs and GNs, exhibit lower crystallinity levels, consistent with our earlier studies [21,22,47].

Fig. 3(a) and (b) shows the FE-SEM photographs of the resulting blank and C-012 coating onto the wooden substrates, respectively. A noticeable distinction is observed in surface characteristics between the blank sample, which exhibits a smooth texture, and the C012 sample, which displays a roughened surface. This rough texture can be primarily attributed to the inclusion of CN + CNT + GN composites. The roughness is predominantly a result of the presence of both CNT and GN, contributing to a high surface-to-volume ratio and, consequently, a significantly increased accessible surface area, which manifests as protrusions on the surface coating. To inspect the elemental distribution, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was employed to determine which chemical elements are present in a sample, and can be used to estimate their relative abundance. The EDS analysis reveals elemental distributions, which are presented in Fig. 3. Notably, Si, Na, and O elements exhibit a uniform dispersion across the wooden substrate, owing to the presence of the primary component, sodium metasilicate. In the case of the C-012 coating, we observe not only the even distribution of Si, Na, and O elements but also the consistent presence of carbon (C) on the substrate. This can be attributed to the incorporation of carbon composites into the base coating, thereby creating a three-dimensional fire-resistant framework.

Fig. 4 provides a set of digital photographs showcasing fireproof coatings filled with 9 wt% carbon composites on wooden substrates, with the wooden substrates having been subjected to heating at 1050 °C for 10 s. In these images, all fireproof coatings exhibit evident signs of charring on their surfaces. It is worth noting that the extent of charring and carbonization is a critical metric for evaluating flame retardancy. Upon heating to 1050 °C for 10 s, the C-012 coating exhibits the smallest proportion of carbonization when compared to the other samples. Following the flame injection test, both the C-01 and C-02 coatings display numerous raised areas in the carbonized region, whereas the C-12 sample appears to have undergone more extensive burning. This underscores the fact that the well-conceived design of carbon composites plays a substantial role in mitigating flame propagation and charring, ultimately leading to reduced carbonization areas.

To inspect the flame retardancy, TGA was adopted to provide quantitative decomposition information on a composite material and can be used to investigate degradation kinetics and char formation [51,52]. The weight loss was closely monitored throughout the chemical decomposition process in an air environment, as depicted in Fig. 5 . The TGA curves for all the samples consistently exhibited three distinct weight-loss stages: (i) dehydration (100–200 °C): this stage primarily involves the release of water vapor from the mixture of gypsum and sodium metasilicate, (ii) ignition and decomposition (200–350 °C): during this phase, the materials undergo ignition and subsequent decomposition, and (iii) pyrolysis and degradation (550–700 °C): this stage involves further pyrolysis and degradation of the materials. Across all the samples, it is noteworthy that the primary weight loss occurs during the dehydration stage (i), as water vapor is liberated from the gypsum + sodium metasilicate mixture. Importantly, the presence of CN + CNT + GN composites notably delays the onset of maximal decomposition. The order of maximal derivative weight can be outlined as C-012 (~175 °C) > C-01 and C-02 (~120 °C) > C-12 (~100 °C). It is a well-recognized principle that the effectiveness of flame retardant materials can be assessed by analyzing the volatiles and the resulting residue as the temperature increases. In comparison, the C-012 coating demonstrates the highest residual weight, approximately 70.5 wt%, after thermal oxidation at 800 °C. Based on these experimental findings, the introduction of a carbon composite coating substantially enhances the fire resistance of the construction coating. This improved residue yield and reduction in mass loss rate can be attributed to the addition of the carbon composite coating, which induces the creation of an effective barrier and insulating layer within the composite. It is believed that this carbon composite coating establishes “tortuous channels” effectively inhibiting the release of volatile products and facilitating mass and heat exchange control during the thermal degradation process [53,54].

Fig. 6 depicts the evolution of carbonization fractions over time for all fireproof coatings applied to wooden substrates. For the sake of comparison, the carbonization fraction of the blank sample, without any carbon composites, is also presented. Remarkably, the blank coating appears to undergo complete carbonization after 60 s of exposure to the flame injection, highlighting its vulnerability to fire. In stark contrast, the introduction of carbon composites clearly mitigates the extent of carbonization, underscoring their efficacy as highly efficient flame retardants. As anticipated, the C-012 coating emerges as the frontrunner in terms of fire resistance among the examined samples. As observed in Fig. 4, the carbon composites, in conjunction with sodium metasilicate + gypsum residues, conjoin to create a tightly-woven char layer characterized by reduced holes and smaller cracks. This resulting composite film serves as a robust physical barrier that curtails mass and heat exchange between the condensed and gas phases while inhibiting the escape of volatile substances. Consequently, this leads to a significant enhancement in flame retardancy and smoke suppression [55]. Indeed, the carbon composite framework operates as a protective and insulating shield, restricting the diffusion of heat and oxygen while safeguarding the underlying wooden substrate [56]. Given its simplicity, ease of production, scalability, and cost-effectiveness, we firmly believe that this fireproof coating holds substantial promise for large-scale applications. Furthermore, our methodology can readily be adapted to create composite thin films on a variety of other substrates, thereby facilitating the development of novel flame retardant coatings across diverse applications in construction and beyond.

Fig. 7 illustrates the shielding effect on electromagnetic and electric fields by both the blank and various carbon composite coatings. In this experiment, an electrical appliance operating at a utility frequency of 60 Hz emitted an electric field of 350 V/m and a magnetic field of 43 × 10−6 T, serving as the reference background. Initially, upon covering with the blank coating, both the magnetic and electric fields experienced a slight reduction to 39 × 10−6 T and 225 V/m, respectively. In stark contrast, the carbon coatings exhibited a substantial reduction in both the electromagnetic and electric fields. Relative to the original magnetic source, the EMI shielding effectiveness followed the order of C-012 (54.7 %) > C-02 (48.8 %) > C-01 (41.9 %) > C-12 (36.0 %) > blank coating (9.3 %). Furthermore, when assessed in decibels (dB), the sequence can be expressed as follows: C-012 (−3.43 dB) > C-02 (−2.91 dB) > C-01 (−2.35 dB) > C-12 (−1.91 dB) > blank coating (−0.42 dB). The EMI shielding performance also showed a similar sequence with the reduction in magnetic field, as depicted in Fig. 8(a). Fig. S1 illustrates the EMI shielding process in terms of absorption, reflection, and numerous internal reflections in carbon composite. Notably, this outcome underscores the significant enhancement in the EMI shielding capacity of the construction coating attributed to the incorporation of the carbon composite coating.

Fig. 7 illustrates the shielding effect on electromagnetic and electric fields by both the blank and various carbon composite coatings. In this experiment, an electrical appliance operating at a utility frequency of 60 Hz emitted an electric field of 350 V/m and a magnetic field of 43 × 10−6 T, serving as the reference background. Initially, upon covering with the blank coating, both the magnetic and electric fields experienced a slight reduction to 39 × 10−6 T and 225 V/m, respectively. In stark contrast, the carbon coatings exhibited a substantial reduction in both the electromagnetic and electric fields. Relative to the original magnetic source, the EMI shielding effectiveness followed the order of C-012 (54.7 %) > C-02 (48.8 %) > C-01 (41.9 %) > C-12 (36.0 %) > blank coating (9.3 %). Furthermore, when assessed in decibels (dB), the sequence can be expressed as follows: C-012 (−3.43 dB) > C-02 (−2.91 dB) > C-01 (−2.35 dB) > C-12 (−1.91 dB) > blank coating (−0.42 dB). The EMI shielding performance also showed a similar sequence with the reduction in magnetic field, as depicted in Fig. 8(a). Fig. S1 illustrates the EMI shielding process in terms of absorption, reflection, and numerous internal reflections in carbon composite. Notably, this outcome underscores the significant enhancement in the EMI shielding capacity of the construction coating attributed to the incorporation of the carbon composite coating.

The enhanced EMI shielding performance achieved through the utilization of the carbon composite coating can be attributed to a synergistic effect that involves both reflection-loss phenomena within the carbon composites. This effect can be categorized into two main types: the first type is a result of resonant behaviors arising from the presence of the CN + CNT + GN interface, while the other type stems from wavelength attenuation (reduction of the amplitude of a signal) [57]. It is widely recognized that both CNTs and GNs exhibit metal-like properties characterized by low electrical resistivity, rendering them strong reflectors of electromagnetic waves [58]. On the flip side, functionalized CNs can be considered optical semiconductors capable of emitting highly intense blue light (~430 nm) when exposed to ultraviolet illumination (~360 nm). This property imparts an optical band gap of 2.88 eV [21,22]. Consequently, semiconductor-type CNs, enriched with oxygen and nitrogen functionalities, serve as multifunctional fillers that not only structurally design but also chemically modify the EMI wave absorption performance of carbon coatings. In general, achieving an optimal resistance and dielectric loss is beneficial for enhancing the electromagnetic wave absorption capacity of a material [59]. Hence, the inclusion of CN fillers within CNTs (C-01), GNs (C-02), and CNT + GN (C-012) composites plays a pivotal role in tuning the resistance and dielectric loss, thereby facilitating electromagnetic absorption by carbon composites. This explains why C-01, C-02, and C-012 coatings outperform the C-12 coating, as illustrated in Fig. 7. Fig. 8(b) showcases the influence of layer thickness on the EMI shielding performance of the C-012 coating. Notably, an increase in layer thickness exhibits a positive effect on improving EMI shielding performance, primarily stemming from the heightened electromagnetic absorption capabilities of carbon composites. Based on these experimental findings, it becomes evident that the carbon composite (0D + 1D + 2D) framework substantially enhances the electromagnetic wave absorption performance of construction coatings.

This work demonstrated the feasibility of carbon composite coating, consisting of CNs (0D), CNTs (1D), and GNs (2D nanomaterials), with sodium metasilicate + gypsum matrix, for flame retardancy and EMI shielding applications. The resulting carbon coatings on wooden plates displayed superior fireproof performance based on the experimental results of fire injection at 1050 °C. This underscores the fact that the well-conceived design of carbon composites plays a substantial role in mitigating flame propagation and charring, ultimately leading to reduced carbonization areas. TGA analysis of the carbon composite coating revealed that high mass of char residue can be obtained at 800 °C, indicating the enhanced thermal insulating performance through the robust design of carbon composite architectures. The carbon coatings also exhibited a substantial reduction in both the electromagnetic and electric fields, showing a satisfactory EMI shielding effect. This improved EMI shielding performance could be attributed to the inclusion of CN fillers within CNT + GN composites plays a pivotal role in tuning the resistance and dielectric loss, thereby facilitating electromagnetic absorption by carbon composites. The order of EMI shielding performance relative to the magnetic source was C-012 (54.7 %) > C-02 (48.8 %) > C-01 (41.9 %) > C-12 (36.0 %) > blank coating (9.3 %). Accordingly, the carbonaceous composites could be engineered as not only fire-retardant fillers but also EMI absorbers through the robust design of carbon composite coating. Such a significant improvement in flame retardancy and EMI shielding demonstrated that the carbon composites are excellent additives to flame-retardant and EMI-adsorbing construction coating, considering their high efficiency, pollutant reduction, non-toxicity, low cost, and environmental friendliness.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
EMI shielding process of carbon composite.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2024.108628.

Tsung-Pin Tasi: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Min-Wei Wu: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Pranjyan Dash: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology. Chien-Te Hsieh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan, provided through grant numbers NSTC 112-2221-E-155-005-MY3.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. de la Roche, J. A. Dangerfield, E. Karacabeyli, Wood Products and Sustainable Construction, New Zel. Timber Des. J. 12 (2003) 9−13.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wood fireproofing coatings based on biobased phenolic resins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.L. de Hoyos-Martínez, H. Issaoui, R. Herrera, J. Labidi, F.C.-E. Bouhtoury, Wood Fireproofing Coatings Based on Biobased Phenolic Resins, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 9 (2021) 1729−1740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treatments and modification to improve the reaction to fire of wood and wood based products–an overview
